Strategic Intelligence Report

Mobile viewport analysis — https://www.awdmods.com/

Evidence Confidence HIGH

Where to Start

5 focused changes that fix most of what's holding this page back. Tackle these first — everything else can wait.

[01]

Redesign the hero — add a headline, H1, and trust signal

HIGH
Fixes 5 findings across 4 clustersHeadlines & ButtonsSearch FindabilityTrust & CredibilityMobile Experience

The hero fills the entire above-fold area with a vehicle fitment selector and a car photo, but has no headline, no H1, no subheadline, and no trust signal. A first-time visitor cannot answer "what does this store sell and why should I buy here?" within 5 seconds. The title tag ("AWDMods – AWDMods") and missing meta description compound the problem — Google has no useful signals to generate SERP snippets. This is the single highest-impact change on the page.

▸Do this:Add an H1 headline above the fitment selector ("Performance Parts for Your WRX, Focus RS & STI"), a one-line subheadline ("Free shipping $75+ · Curated fitment guaranteed"), and a compact trust line ("★★★★★ Rated by 2,400+ enthusiasts"). Set the Shopify title tag to match the H1 and add a 150-character meta description.
Underlying findings
  • Headlines & Buttonsvisual-cta F-01→
  • Headlines & Buttonsvisual-cta F-02→
  • Headlines & Buttonsvisual-cta F-08→
  • Search Findabilitycontent-seo F-01→
  • Search Findabilitycontent-seo F-02→
  • Search Findabilitycontent-seo F-03→
  • Trust & Credibilitytrust-credibility F-02→
[02]

Add a sticky bottom navigation bar

HIGH
Fixes 4 findings across 3 clustersHeadlines & ButtonsMobile ExperienceBrowse & Search

Once a visitor scrolls past the hero, the Find Parts button disappears and the only navigation is a hamburger menu in the top-left corner — the hardest reach zone for one-handed use. Getting to any category page requires 3 taps (hamburger → Shop by Category → category). There is no persistent conversion path following the visitor through the page.

▸Do this:Add a 56px sticky bottom bar with 4 icons: Home, Search, Browse (links to /collections), and Cart (with live count badge). Show it after the user scrolls past the hero. This replaces the hamburger as the primary navigation and keeps a conversion pathway within thumb reach at all times.
Underlying findings
  • Headlines & Buttonsvisual-cta F-07→
  • Mobile Experiencemobile-performance F-05→
  • Mobile Experiencemobile-performance F-06→
  • Browse & Searchcategory-navigation F-02→
[03]

Build review volume past the 5-review threshold

HIGH
Fixes 3 findings across 2 clustersTrust & CredibilityBrowse & Search

Every product card shows a perfect 5.0/5.0 from only 2 reviews. Research shows purchase likelihood peaks at 4.0–4.7 stars and *decreases* approaching 5.0 because shoppers suspect manipulation. Meanwhile, products with 5+ reviews convert at 270% higher rates than products with zero — and with only 2 reviews, the store is capturing almost none of that lift.

▸Do this:Launch a post-purchase review email 7–14 days after delivery, targeting products with fewer than 5 reviews first. Use specific prompts ("How does the fit compare to OEM?") to get detailed, authentic reviews. Don't filter for 5-star only — mixed ratings in the 4.0–4.7 range build more trust than a perfect score.
Underlying findings
  • Trust & Credibilitytrust-credibility F-01→
  • Trust & Credibilitytrust-credibility F-07→
  • Browse & Searchcategory-navigation F-07(unmatched)
[04]

Fix hero image loading and eliminate layout shift

HIGH
Fixes 3 findings across 2 clustersMobile ExperienceSearch Findability

The hero image — the largest visual element on the page — may be lazy-loaded (telling the browser to fetch it last, the opposite of what you want). Images throughout the page lack explicit width and height attributes, causing the page to jump around as images load. And the hero image may be a CSS background, making it completely invisible to Google Images and Google Lens.

▸Do this:Add `fetchpriority="high"` to the hero image tag, add `width` and `height` attributes to every image on the page, and verify the hero is an `<img>` element (not a CSS `background-image`) with descriptive alt text.
Underlying findings
  • Mobile Experiencemobile-performance F-01→
  • Mobile Experiencemobile-performance F-03→
  • Search Findabilitycontent-seo F-04→
[05]

Surface trust signals near the fold — payment icons, return policy, contact info

MEDIUM
Fixes 4 findings across 1 clusterTrust & Credibility

Payment icons (Amex, Apple Pay, Google Pay, etc.) sit in the footer — visible only after scrolling past the entire page. There's no return policy mentioned anywhere, no money-back guarantee, and no contact information visible in the mobile flow without opening the hamburger menu. For "Made to Order" products, the absence of a clear return policy is especially anxiety-inducing.

▸Do this:Add a single trust strip row below the hero: payment icons + "30-Day Returns" + "Contact Us" link. One row, three signals, one implementation.
Underlying findings
  • Trust & Credibilitytrust-credibility F-03→
  • Trust & Credibilitytrust-credibility F-04→
  • Trust & Credibilitytrust-credibility F-05→
  • Trust & Credibilitytrust-credibility F-06→
Primary Interface Evidence
Diagnostic Insights
1 High
VISUAL

Hero Layout

The hero fills the entire above-fold area with a four-dropdown vehicle fitment tool and a background photo of a car in a nighttime cityscape, but there is no headline or subheadline anywhere on the screen. A first-time visitor lands and sees dropdowns with no text telling them what AWDMods sells, who it's for, or why they should care.

Recommendation

Add a one-line headline above the fitment selector — something like "Performance Parts for Your WRX, Focus RS & STI" — and a one-line subheadline below it. Keep both under 10 words each so they fit cleanly on mobile. The headline should be in an H1 tag for SEO.

The first thing a visitor needs to know is "is this site for me?" Without a headline, the answer takes too long to form. Baymard research shows five elements need to be above the fold: headline, subheadline, CTA, hero image, and trust signal. Right now three of five are missing.
Hero Section Psychology Gold
View Source
2 High
VISUAL

Above Fold Content

The hero has no visible trust signal above the fold — no star rating, no review count, no "used by X customers," no media mention. The only trust-adjacent element is the free shipping banner, which communicates shipping policy but not whether other customers have bought here and been happy. Also identified by trust-credibility cluster.

Recommendation

Add a compact trust line just below the hero headline: "★★★★★ Rated by 2,400+ performance enthusiasts" or equivalent. Keep it a single line.

Cold visitors have no existing reason to trust AWDMods over any other parts retailer. Stanford Web Credibility research found users assess site legitimacy within seconds. A single star rating or customer count is the minimum viable trust signal.
Hero Section Psychology Gold
View Source
3 Medium
VISUAL

Mobile Cta

The "FIND PARTS" button only becomes active after all four dropdowns are filled. A visitor who wants to browse before committing to a vehicle search has no alternative path — the hero gives them nothing to do except fill out the form.

Recommendation

Add a secondary text-link CTA below the "Find Parts" button: "Browse all parts →" or "Shop without a vehicle." Style it as a text link so it doesn't compete with the primary CTA.

The fitment selector is right for high-intent visitors, but a significant portion are still in discovery mode. If the only option requires filling four dropdowns, those visitors bounce rather than explore.
Hero Section Psychology Bronze
View Source
4 Medium
VISUAL

Mobile Cta

The category card "SHOP [CATEGORY]" buttons appear to be in the 36–40px range, below the recommended 48px minimum for mobile touch targets.

Recommendation

Increase height to at minimum 48px. Change the label from all-caps to title case ("Shop Performance") to improve readability.

Touch targets below 48px are measurably harder to tap accurately on mobile. Google's Material Design and Apple's HIG both set 48px as the practical minimum.
CTA Design And Placement Silver
View Source
5 Medium
VISUAL

Mobile Cta

Product cards show a small orange circular "+" button overlaid on the product image thumbnail, approximately 32–36px in diameter — well below the 48px minimum.

Recommendation

Increase to at minimum 48px diameter, or replace with a dedicated "Quick Add" text button below each product card.

Touch targets too small are one of the most common and easiest-to-fix conversion killers on mobile. Below 48px, miss-taps increase significantly.
CTA Design And Placement Silver
View Source
6 High
VISUAL

Mobile Cta

There is no sticky bottom CTA bar anywhere on the page. Once a visitor scrolls past the hero, the "Find Parts" button disappears entirely. There is no persistent conversion opportunity following them through the page. Also identified by mobile-performance cluster.

Recommendation

Implement a sticky bottom bar on mobile, 56–60px tall, with a compact "Find Parts" button and a "Shop All" text link. Reveal it only after the user scrolls past the hero.

A/B tests on Shopify stores show sticky bottom CTAs increase conversions by 5–37%. GrowthRock found +7.9% completed orders at 99% statistical significance.
CTA Design And Placement Bronze
View Source
7 High
VISUAL

Visual Hierarchy

Applying the squint test — squinting until detail blurs — reveals three elements competing at roughly equal visual weight: the dropdown selectors, the "Find Parts" button, and the background car image. Nothing clearly dominates. The absence of any headline text leaves no text element to anchor the visual hierarchy.

Recommendation

Establish a clear one-two-three hierarchy: (1) large white headline, (2) fitment dropdowns, (3) high-contrast "Find Parts" button. Add a semi-transparent dark overlay on the hero image.

When multiple elements compete at equal weight, visitors experience micro-paralysis — a brief moment of uncertainty that makes the first impression feel cluttered. Clear visual hierarchy increases both engagement time and conversion.
Hero Section Psychology Silver
View Source
8 Low
VISUAL

Cta Copy

The "FIND PARTS" CTA is written from the store's perspective rather than the visitor's. There is also no benefit embedded in the button copy.

Recommendation

Test "Search My Vehicle" or "Find Parts for My [Make]" (dynamically updated once Make is selected). Add a short benefit line under the button: "Guaranteed fitment · Free shipping $75+".

First-person CTA copy ("Get my results" vs "Get your results") showed a 90% lift in the most-cited A/B test.
Headline Copywriting Bronze
View Source
9 High
BOTH

Star Ratings

Every product card displaying a star rating shows a perfect 5.0 out of 5.0, with only 2 total reviews. A flawless score from just two people is the opposite of reassuring — consumers have learned to read a perfect 5-star count on two reviews as either fabricated or so new that real feedback doesn't exist yet.

Recommendation

Until a product has at least 5 reviews, consider not displaying the star rating on the product card. Prioritize a post-purchase email flow requesting reviews from buyers, targeting products under 5 reviews first.

Northwestern University's Spiegel Research Center found purchase likelihood peaks between 4.0 and 4.7 stars and actually falls as ratings approach 5.0. Products with 5 or more reviews convert at 270% higher rates than those with near-zero reviews.
Trust And Credibility Gold
View Source
10 High
VISUAL

Trust Above Fold

The entire above-fold area contains zero trust signals. No review count, no star rating, no guarantee, no security badges, no press mentions. A first-time visitor has no immediate evidence that this is a trustworthy place to spend money.

Recommendation

Add a slim trust bar directly below the announcement bar or hero, containing: a star rating with total review count, a return/guarantee statement ("30-Day Returns"), and your payment icons.

Baymard's behavioral study found trust badges near conversion points lift conversion by 15–32% for unknown brands. Leaving the above-fold area signal-free means visitors are making trust judgments with no evidence.
Trust And Credibility Gold
View Source
11 Medium
VISUAL

Trust Badges

Payment method icons (Amex, Apple Pay, Discover, Google Pay, Mastercard, PayPal, Shop Pay) are visible only in the footer below everything. A shopper deciding whether to add something to cart sees no payment trust signals until after they've already scrolled past the entire page.

Recommendation

Duplicate payment icons into a trust strip placed above the fold. Keep the footer version too.

Baymard found badges in headers and footers are perceived as generic decoration. Badges near the conversion moment are the ones that move purchase decisions — 12.2% conversion lift from moving trust badges adjacent to the purchase action.
Trust And Credibility Gold
View Source
12 Medium
BOTH

Trust Badges

No return policy, money-back guarantee, or "Made to Order" explanation visible anywhere. Multiple "Made to Order" badges appear on product cards with no explanation of lead time or returnability. For first-time visitors, "Made to Order" without context reads as "this will take a long time and may not be returnable."

Recommendation

Add an explanation link to the "Made to Order" badge ("Made to Order — 3-5 business day lead time, fully returnable") and a return policy badge near the featured collection.

77% of shoppers base purchase decisions on the return policy (Signifyd 2024). The "Made to Order" badge without context creates anxiety — shoppers assume the worst unless told otherwise.
Trust And Credibility Bronze
View Source
13 Medium
BOTH

Reviews Display

Product cards display star ratings but no "Verified Purchase" or "Verified Buyer" label. For a brand unknown to first-time visitors, unverified-appearing ratings carry less weight.

Recommendation

Enable "Verified Purchase" badges on review displays sitewide. Even a subtle "(2 verified reviews)" label increases credibility.

Spiegel Research Center measured a 15% purchase likelihood increase when reviews show "Verified Buyer" vs. anonymous.
Trust And Credibility Gold
View Source
14 Medium
BOTH

Social Proof Placement

No visible phone number, email address, or physical address anywhere in the mobile homepage flow. Contact info is buried behind the hamburger menu. No "About AWDMods" blurb is surfaced inline.

Recommendation

Surface at minimum one contact channel in the footer without requiring menu interaction. Add a brief "About AWDMods" line or year-founded stat.

NNGroup's trust research across 350+ sites found users' most common question is "who is behind this business?" Google Quality Rater Guidelines treat absence of contact info as a Trustworthiness failure signal.
Trust And Credibility Gold
View Source
15 High
BOTH

Review Count

Multiple featured products have only 2 reviews each. One card appears to have zero reviews. Products below the 5-review threshold are in the lowest-conversion band — behaving almost identically to products with zero reviews.

Recommendation

Launch a systematic post-purchase review collection email targeting every buyer of products with fewer than 5 reviews. Use specific-outcome prompts and consider a review incentive with FTC-compliant disclosure.

Products with 5 reviews convert at 270% higher rates than zero reviews (Spiegel). For high-ticket automotive parts, the multiplier is 380%.
Social Proof Patterns Gold
View Source
16 High
CODE

Value Proposition

The page title reads "AWDMods – AWDMods" — the brand name listed twice with no vehicle names, no product category, no reason for a shopper to click from a Google results page.

Recommendation

Set the Shopify homepage title to "AWDMods – Performance Parts for Focus RS, WRX & STI" (52 characters). Lead with the brand, then name the product category and vehicle targets.

Google rewrites over 60% of page titles. A title this thin gives Google nothing useful to work with. Shoppers scanning Google results read the first two or three words most carefully — leading with a vehicle name signals relevance immediately.
Title Formulas SERP Psychology Silver
View Source
17 High
CODE

Value Proposition

The page has no meta description at all. Google is generating the snippet text itself — typically pulling from the vehicle finder widget labels rather than meaningful selling copy.

Recommendation

Add a meta description of 140–160 characters: "AWDMods stocks performance, exterior, and handling parts for Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX, and STI. Find your fit — free shipping over $75."

While meta descriptions aren't a direct ranking factor, they're the snippet shoppers see on Google. Auto-generated snippets from widget labels consistently underperform crafted descriptions.
Title Formulas SERP Psychology Gold
View Source
18 High
CODE

Value Proposition

No H1 element appears anywhere in the homepage DOM. The hero uses the vehicle finder widget; the newsletter section uses an H2. Without an H1, Google has no authoritative on-page heading signal, and it's also a WCAG accessibility requirement.

Recommendation

Add an H1 to the homepage — either as a visually prominent headline above the fitment selector or styled to match visual hierarchy while maintaining correct HTML semantics. Align H1 text with the title tag.

Google's title-rewrite algorithm uses the H1 as a primary alternative signal when the title tag is insufficient. When neither carries keyword signals, Google has no authoritative source.
Title Formulas SERP Psychology Gold
View Source
19 Medium
BOTH

Hero Layout

The hero shows a white WRX/STI against a nighttime cityscape. The DOM slice doesn't include this image's tag — it may be a CSS background rather than an img element. If so, it's completely invisible to Google Images and Google Lens (4 billion monthly shopping visual searches).

Recommendation

Confirm the hero image is an img element, not a CSS background. Add descriptive alt text: "Subaru WRX with aftermarket performance parts — AWDMods."

If served as a CSS background, the hero receives zero SEO or accessibility value. 3,948 ADA website lawsuits were filed in 2025, with 91.5% targeting ecommerce.
Image SEO Alt Text Gold
View Source
20 Medium
CODE

Value Proposition

The page has OnlineStore and WebSite schema but no Organization entity with name, URL, logo, contactPoint, or sameAs (social profiles). The footer confirms AWDMods has active Facebook, Instagram, and YouTube accounts.

Recommendation

Add an Organization JSON-LD block with name, URL, logo, and sameAs array pointing to social profiles.

Organization schema feeds Google's Knowledge Panel and AI shopping agents. Missing sameAs weakens brand entity signals.
Schema Product Markup Gold
View Source
21 Low
CODE

Value Proposition

The newsletter form has no visible consent disclosure, privacy policy link, or message frequency notice.

Recommendation

Add a single line below the email input: "By subscribing, you agree to receive marketing emails. Privacy Policy" (linked).

Reduces user confidence and risks GDPR non-compliance for EU visitors.
Ethics Gate Gold
(source unavailable)
22 High
CODE

Page Load

The hero vehicle image is the largest above-fold visual and almost certainly the Largest Contentful Paint element. If it carries loading="lazy" or lacks fetchpriority="high", it directly worsens the time until the visitor sees something meaningful. Google requires this metric under 2.5 seconds for a "good" score.

Recommendation

Remove loading="lazy" from the hero image and add fetchpriority="high". The correct pattern: <img fetchpriority="high" loading="eager">.

A controlled A/B test at Vodafone showed a 31% improvement in this metric produced 8% more online sales. Lazy-loading the image a visitor sees first tells the browser to fetch it last.
Media Performance Optimization Gold
View Source
23 Medium
CODE

Image Optimization

The newsletter image is served with srcset widths up to 3840px to a 390px mobile screen, and the filename ends in .png — not .webp or .avif. WebP images are 25–34% smaller; AVIF is 45–55% smaller.

Recommendation

Verify mobile visitors receive WebP via Shopify's CDN. Use image_url with format: 'webp' in Liquid. Target hero image under 100KB at mobile viewport.

A shopper on a 4G connection in a parking lot is more likely to bounce if images are multi-hundred-KB PNGs when a 60KB WebP would do the same job.
Media Performance Optimization Gold
View Source
24 High
CODE

Image Optimization

Images lack explicit width and height attributes, causing the page to jump around as images load and claim their space. This causes misclicks — a user taps "Shop Performance" and the page shifts, triggering an accidental tap on a different element.

Recommendation

Add width and height attributes to every img element. Add CSS: img { max-width: 100%; height: auto; }

Layout shift makes the page feel broken. Swappie reduced layout shift by 91% and saw a 42% increase in mobile revenue. The fix — two HTML attributes per image — is free and immediate.
Media Performance Optimization Gold
View Source
25 Medium
CODE

Mobile Touch Targets

The menu hamburger and cart icon measure approximately 46px — borderline at/slightly below the 48px Google Material Design minimum. At 3x DPR, the 139×139 device pixel measurement resolves to ~46px CSS.

Recommendation

Increase padding on both icons to confirm a minimum 48px × 48px tap target. Verify 8px spacing between adjacent targets.

Touch targets smaller than 44px have 3x higher error rates (University of Maryland). A mis-tap on the cart button when the user meant the menu is a conversion killer.
Mobile Conversion Silver
View Source
26 High
VISUAL

Mobile Nav

The header uses a hamburger menu in the top-left corner — the hardest reach zone for one-handed users. 49% of mobile users hold their phone one-handed. There is no persistent bottom navigation bar. Also identified by category-navigation cluster.

Recommendation

Add a persistent sticky bottom navigation bar with 4 destinations: Home, Search, Shop/Browse, and Cart with live item count. The hamburger can remain for secondary navigation.

Navigation that requires a stretch to the top of the screen is a friction tax on every single page visit. Replacing hamburger-only with a visible bottom bar increases engagement 25–50%.
Mobile Conversion Gold
View Source

VISUAL

Mobile Cta

As the user scrolls past the hero into product listings, there is no persistent call-to-action keeping a next step visible. The product grid shows pricing but no visible Add to Cart button on cards.

Recommendation

Implement a lightweight sticky bottom bar that appears once the user scrolls past the hero.

Mobile shoppers scroll quickly and lose their place. Without a sticky element, momentum breaks and they leave without acting.

28 Medium
VISUAL

Mobile Scroll

The category tiles carousel shows only 1 full tile and a partial sliver of the next. No dots, progress indicator, or swipe hint indicates more categories exist off-screen. Users who miss the partial peek won't know Interior, Exterior, and Electronics exist.

Recommendation

Add pagination dots or a "swipe for more" label beneath the carousel. Alternatively, reduce tile width so 2 full tiles are always visible.

If shoppers don't know more categories exist off-screen, they only see half the store. 40% of users in Baymard testing failed to locate navigation options when not prominently visible.
Mobile Conversion Gold
View Source
29 Medium
VISUAL

Mobile Font Size

If the vehicle selector dropdown font is below 16px, iOS Safari will auto-zoom the form — disrupting the fitment flow at exactly the moment the user is engaging.

Recommendation

Set font-size: 16px on all select, input, and textarea elements. Use CSS font-size: max(16px, 1rem) as a safeguard.

When a user taps a dropdown and the page zooms in because the font is too small, the entire form is disrupted. Trivially preventable with one CSS rule.
Mobile Conversion Silver
View Source
30 Medium
CODE

Page Load

Two fonts are preloaded (Roboto Condensed, Open Sans Condensed) — good practice. However, without font-display: swap, the browser may hold text invisible for up to 3 seconds on slower connections. Without size-adjusted fallback metrics, the swap causes layout shift.

Recommendation

Set font-display: swap on both font declarations. Add size-adjust, ascent-override, descent-override CSS descriptors to minimize layout shift on swap.

A page that goes blank for 2 seconds while fonts load looks broken. font-display: swap costs nothing and makes the page feel significantly faster.
Core Web Vitals Silver
View Source
31 Low
CODE

Page Load

No Speculation Rules API is implemented. This Chrome feature allows prerendering pages a visitor is likely to navigate to next — so tapping a product card loads it instantly. Ray-Ban saw a 101% increase in mobile product page conversion.

Recommendation

Add a Speculation Rules script that prerenders the top 3–5 collection pages. Use eagerness: "moderate" (prerender on hover) to avoid wasting mobile bandwidth.

Making page transitions feel instant is the biggest conversion lift from performance. Chrome-only (~65% of mobile traffic) but degrades gracefully.
Core Web Vitals Silver
View Source
32 Medium
VISUAL

Price Anchoring

Product cards show only a single "From $135.99" price with no strikethrough anchor, no MSRP, no "compare at" value. Without a reference price, shoppers have no anchor to judge whether $135.99 is a good deal.

Recommendation

For products with a real MSRP or prior selling price, display it as a strikethrough: "~~$159.99~~ From $135.99". Only use prices genuinely charged previously — FTC 16 CFR Part 233 requires reference prices to reflect real prior pricing.

When no reference price is shown, the anchor defaults to whatever the shopper already believes — often a competitor's lower price. Real-world A/B tests report 5–15% conversion lifts from adding credible strikethrough pricing.
Price Anchoring Gold
View Source
33 Medium
VISUAL

Price Display

All product cards use "From $X" pricing with no indication of which configuration drives the final price. The "Made to Order" badge adds ambiguity — visitors can't tell if MTO means a price premium.

Recommendation

Add a secondary line beneath "From $X" naming the lowest configuration (e.g., "From $135.99 — Driver side only").

"From $X" with no context creates uncertainty that discourages clicks — shoppers fear the real price will be much higher.
Pricing Psychology Bronze
View Source
34 Medium
BOTH

Price Display

No BNPL installment pricing is displayed anywhere. For $135.99+ products on mobile, showing "4 payments of $34.00" would be the most salient form of price reduction available.

Recommendation

Add a Shop Pay Installments widget below product prices on cards or product pages. Display as "4 interest-free payments of $34.00" with the provider logo.

A field study of 275,000 purchases found BNPL increases basket sizes by 10%. Displaying it on the product page drives 30–50% more BNPL adoption vs. checkout-only.
BNPL Payment Gold
View Source
35 Medium
BOTH

Price Display

No savings amount, discount percentage, or promotional framing on any product card. Neither card communicates whether the price represents a sale or regular price.

Recommendation

For products on sale, display the original price (strikethrough) and savings: "~~$159.99~~ $135.99 — Save $24." For items over $100, use dollar savings (not percentage) per the Rule of 100.

How a discount is displayed is nearly as important as the amount. Dollar-off framing generates higher perceived deal value for products over $100.
Discount Framing Gold
View Source
36 Low
VISUAL

Shipping Cost Display

The free shipping banner reads "FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+" — the word "most" introduces ambiguity. Shoppers can't know which orders are excluded.

Recommendation

If the exclusion set is small (oversize freight only), simplify to "FREE SHIPPING on orders $75+" without "most." Or add a tooltip linking to details.

"Free" triggers a qualitatively different psychological response — the zero-price effect — but only when unambiguous. "Most" reintroduces uncertainty.
Discount Framing Gold
View Source
37 High
BOTH

Category Navigation

The homepage category tiles section uses a horizontal carousel where only 1 full tile and a partial sliver of the next are visible. No dots, progress indicator, or swipe-hint text indicates more categories exist. Users who miss the partial peek won't discover Interior, Exterior, and Electronics.

Recommendation

Add pagination dots or reduce tile width so 2 full tiles plus a partial third are always visible. Or use a 2-column grid to surface all 5 categories without requiring swipe.

40% of users in Baymard testing failed to locate navigation options when not prominently visible. For a parts store where category browsing is a primary discovery path, hiding 3 of 5 categories reduces category page traffic.
Search And Filter UX Gold
View Source
38 High
CODE

Mobile Nav

Getting to any category page requires: (1) tap hamburger, (2) tap "Shop by Category," (3) tap the category. That's 3 taps with a slide-in submenu pattern adding cognitive load. Top-level category links (direct from the open drawer) are absent.

Recommendation

Promote the 5 main categories to the top level of the drawer as direct links. This reduces the path from 3 taps to 2.

Each additional navigation step causes measurable drop-off on mobile. NNGroup research shows this is especially true on phones where tap targets are smaller and menus are disorienting.
Collection Page Architecture Gold
View Source
39 High
BOTH

Mobile Nav

"Shop by Vehicle" — the compatibility-first path for automotive parts buyers — is buried in the third position of the drawer menu (after "Shop All" and "Shop by Category"). For customers who know their car but not their category, this is the most intent-matched path, but it requires 2 taps to reach.

Recommendation

Elevate "Shop by Vehicle" to the first position in the drawer. Add a persistent "Find parts for your [vehicle]" shortcut below the search bar or in the header.

Only 35% of users successfully find compatible products on sites with compatibility-dependent catalogs when the compatibility filter isn't prominently surfaced (Baymard). AWDMods' vehicle selector IS the compatibility filter — burying it understates its importance.
Search And Filter UX Gold
View Source

BOTH

Category Navigation

Possible mismatch between drawer menu categories (4 links) and carousel tiles (3 captured in DOM slice). "Electronics" appears in the page context but has no corresponding collection link. Inconsistent navigation creates user disorientation.

Recommendation

Verify all 5 categories have tiles AND drawer links. If Electronics lacks a Shopify collection, create one or remove references.

Navigation inconsistency — where the drawer exposes categories the carousel doesn't — splits user discovery paths unpredictably.

BOTH

Category Navigation

Category tiles list subcategories (Intakes, Exhaust, Cooling, etc.) but show no product count, brand count, or scope indicator. A visitor can't tell if a category has 3 products or 300.

Recommendation

Add a compact scope indicator: "250+ parts" or list top brands. For a niche parts brand, communicating catalog depth is a trust signal.

Users scanning category tiles need to quickly assess whether a category is worth entering. Scope signals reduce decision uncertainty.
Merchandising Psychology Gold
View Source
What’s Working Well
  • cta-placement: The primary "Find Parts" CTA is visible above the fold without scrolling, positioned in natural reading flow.
  • scan-pattern: Single-column carousel layout is appropriate for mobile where F-pattern left-side dominance doesn't apply.
  • shipping-cost-display: Free shipping threshold clearly disclosed in announcement bar before any product pricing is encountered.
  • search-ux: Search bar is persistent, full-width (348px), and 55px tall — exceeds all minimum size requirements.
  • mobile-touch-targets: All header icon tap targets meet 44–46px minimum. Viewport meta allows pinch-to-zoom.
  • CDN delivery: Shopify's Fastly CDN correctly configured with versioned URLs and edge caching.
  • ethics-gate: No dark patterns detected. AI-generated imagery correctly classified as decorative per §5.3. Free shipping banner compliant per §1.2.
E-Commerce Psychology (ECP) v5.0.1
